question_text: Find the centre of mass of three particles at the vertices of an equilateral triangle. The masses of the particles are 100g, 150g, and 200g respectively. Each side of the equilateral triangle is 0.5m long.
question_html: Find the centre of mass of three particles at the vertices of an equilateral triangle. The masses of the particles are 100g, 150g, and 200g respectively. Each side of the equilateral triangle is 0.5m long.
solution_text: {tex}\textbf{Given:}{/tex} Three particles are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side {tex}a = 0.5,\text{m}{/tex} with masses {tex}m_1 = 100,\text{g}, m_2 = 150,\text{g}, m_3 = 200,\text{g}{/tex} {tex}\textbf{Step 1: Coordinate system}{/tex} Choose the vertices as: {tex}A(0,0), B(a,0) = (0.5,0), C\left(\frac{a}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{3}a}{2}\right) = \left(0.25, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\textbf{Step 2: Total mass}{/tex} {tex}M = m_1 + m_2 + m_3 = 100 + 150 + 200 = 450,\text{g}{/tex} {tex}\textbf{Step 3: Centre of mass coordinates}{/tex} {tex}x_{\text{CM}} = \frac{m_1x_1 + m_2x_2 + m_3x_3}{M}{/tex} {tex}x_{\text{CM}} = \frac{100(0) + 150(0.5) + 200(0.25)}{450}{/tex} {tex}x_{\text{CM}} = \frac{75 + 50}{450} = \frac{125}{450} = \frac{5}{18}{/tex} {tex}x_{\text{CM}} = 0.278,\text{m}{/tex} {tex}y_{\text{CM}} = \frac{m_1y_1 + m_2y_2 + m_3y_3}{M}{/tex} {tex}y_{\text{CM}} = \frac{100(0) + 150(0) + 200\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\right)}{450}{/tex} {tex}y_{\text{CM}} = \frac{50\sqrt{3}}{450} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{9}{/tex} {tex}y_{\text{CM}} = 0.192,\text{m}{/tex} Coordinates are measured from the vertex carrying the (100 g) mass, with the base along the (x)-axis.
solution_html:
{tex}\textbf{Given:}{/tex}
Three particles are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side
{tex}a = 0.5,\text{m}{/tex}
with masses
{tex}m_1 = 100,\text{g}, m_2 = 150,\text{g}, m_3 = 200,\text{g}{/tex}

{tex}\textbf{Step 1: Coordinate system}{/tex}

Choose the vertices as:
{tex}A(0,0), B(a,0) = (0.5,0), C\left(\frac{a}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{3}a}{2}\right) = \left(0.25, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\right){/tex}

{tex}\textbf{Step 2: Total mass}{/tex}
{tex}M = m_1 + m_2 + m_3 = 100 + 150 + 200 = 450,\text{g}{/tex}

{tex}\textbf{Step 3: Centre of mass coordinates}{/tex}

{tex}x_{\text{CM}} = \frac{m_1x_1 + m_2x_2 + m_3x_3}{M}{/tex}

{tex}x_{\text{CM}} = \frac{100(0) + 150(0.5) + 200(0.25)}{450}{/tex}

{tex}x_{\text{CM}} = \frac{75 + 50}{450} = \frac{125}{450} = \frac{5}{18}{/tex}

{tex}x_{\text{CM}} = 0.278,\text{m}{/tex}

{tex}y_{\text{CM}} = \frac{m_1y_1 + m_2y_2 + m_3y_3}{M}{/tex}

{tex}y_{\text{CM}} = \frac{100(0) + 150(0) + 200\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\right)}{450}{/tex}

{tex}y_{\text{CM}} = \frac{50\sqrt{3}}{450} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{9}{/tex}

{tex}y_{\text{CM}} = 0.192,\text{m}{/tex}

Coordinates are measured from the vertex carrying the (100 g) mass, with the base along the (x)-axis.

question_text: Find the centre of mass of a triangular lamina.
question_html: Find the centre of mass of a triangular lamina.
solution_text: The lamina ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} LMN) may be subdivided into narrow strips each parallel to the base (MN) as shown in Figure. By symmetry, each strip has its centre of mass at its midpoint. If we join the midpoint of all the strips we get the median LP. The centre of mass of the triangle as a whole, therefore, has to lie on the median LP. Similarly, we can argue that it lies on the median MQ and NR. This means the centre of mass lies on the point of concurrence of the medians. i.e. on the centroid G of the triangle.
solution_html:
The lamina ({tex}\Delta{/tex}LMN) may be subdivided into narrow strips each parallel to the base (MN) as shown in Figure.

By symmetry, each strip has its centre of mass at its midpoint. If we join the midpoint of all the strips we get the median LP. The centre of mass of the triangle as a whole, therefore, has to lie on the median LP. Similarly, we can argue that it lies on the median MQ and NR. This means the centre of mass lies on the point of concurrence of the medians. i.e. on the centroid G of the triangle.

question_html: Find the centre of mass of a uniform L-shaped lamina (a thin flat plate) with dimensions as shown. The mass of the lamina is 3 kg.
solution_text: Choosing the X and Y axes as shown in Figure. we have the coordinates of the vertices of the L-shaped lamina as given in the figure. We can think of the L-shape to consist of 3 squares each of length 1m. The mass of each square is 1kg, since the lamina is uniform. The centres of mass C 1 , C 2 and C 3 of the squares are, by symmetry, their geometric centres and have coordinates ( {tex}\frac 12{/tex} , {tex}\frac 12{/tex} ), ( {tex}\frac 32{/tex} , {tex}\frac 12{/tex} ), ( {tex}\frac 12{/tex} , {tex}\frac 32{/tex} ) respectively. We take the masses of the squares to be concentrated at these points. The centre of mass of the whole L shape (X, Y) is the centre of mass of these mass points. Hence, {tex}X=\frac{[1(1 / 2)+1(3 / 2)+1(1 / 2)] \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}}{(1+1+1) \mathrm{kg}}=\frac{5}{6} \mathrm{m}{/tex} {tex}Y=\frac{[1(1 / 2)+1(1 / 2)+1(3 / 2)] \operatorname{kg} m}{(1+1+1) \mathrm{k}[\mathrm{g}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5}{6} \mathrm{m}{/tex} The centre of mass of the L-shape lies on the line OD. Hence, the centre of the mass of L shape is (​ {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex} )

Choosing the X and Y axes as shown in Figure. we have the coordinates of the vertices of the L-shaped lamina as given in the figure. We can think of the L-shape to consist of 3 squares each of length 1m. The mass of each square is 1kg, since the lamina is uniform. The centres of mass C1, C2 and C3 of the squares are, by symmetry, their geometric centres and have coordinates ({tex}\frac 12{/tex}, {tex}\frac 12{/tex}), ({tex}\frac 32{/tex}, {tex}\frac 12{/tex}), ({tex}\frac 12{/tex}, {tex}\frac 32{/tex}) respectively. We take the masses of the squares to be concentrated at these points. The centre of mass of the whole L shape (X, Y) is the centre of mass of these mass points.

Hence,
{tex}X=\frac{[1(1 / 2)+1(3 / 2)+1(1 / 2)] \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}}{(1+1+1) \mathrm{kg}}=\frac{5}{6} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
{tex}Y=\frac{[1(1 / 2)+1(1 / 2)+1(3 / 2)] \operatorname{kg} m}{(1+1+1) \mathrm{k}[\mathrm{g}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5}{6} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
The centre of mass of the L-shape lies on the line OD.
Hence, the centre of the mass of L shape is (​{tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex})

question_text: Find the torque of a force {tex}7 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-5 \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex} about the origin. The force acts on a particle whose position vector is {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex} .
question_html: Find the torque of a force {tex}7 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-5 \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex} about the origin. The force acts on a particle whose position vector is {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}.
solution_text: Here {tex}\mathbf{\vec r}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\mathbf{\vec F}=7 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-5 \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex} We shall use the determinant rule to find the torque {tex}\tau=\mathbf{\vec r} \times \mathbf{\vec F}{/tex} {tex}\tau=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathbf{i}} & \hat{\mathbf{j}} & \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ 1 & -1 & 1 \\ 7 & 3 & -5 \end{array}\right|=(5-3) \hat{\mathbf{i}}-(-5-7) \hat{\mathbf{j}}{/tex} {tex}+(3-(-7)) \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex} or {tex}\tau=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+12 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+10 \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here {tex}\mathbf{\vec r}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}
and {tex}\mathbf{\vec F}=7 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-5 \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}

question_text: Show that the angular momentum about any point of a single particle moving with constant velocity remains constant throughout the motion.
question_html: Show that the angular momentum about any point of a single particle moving with constant velocity remains constant throughout the motion.
solution_text: Let the particle with velocity v be at point P at some instant t. We want to calculate the angular momentum of the particle about an arbitrary point O. Let us consider that a particle of mass 'm' is moving around in a circular path about point O. The radius of the path is given by the radius vector ' r ' and the linear velocity of the particle is 'v '. Its angular momentum is given as L = r {tex}\times{/tex} mv or L = r {tex}\times{/tex} p Now, we can differentiate the above equation, thus {tex}\frac {dL}{dt} = \frac {d}{dt} (r \times p){/tex} or {tex}\frac {dL}{dt} = r \frac {dp}{dt} + p\frac {dr}{dt}{/tex} or {tex}\frac {dL}{dt} = (r \times F) + p \times v{/tex} Now, the product of p and v will be zero as these vectors are parallel (in the same direction) to each other. thus, {tex}\frac {dL}{dt} = (r \times F) = T{/tex} So, the rate of change of angular momentum would be equal to the torque. Here, as the system is free from outside influence, no external torque applied to the system. This means that τ = 0 or {tex}\frac {dL}{dt}{/tex} = τ = 0 the rate of change of angular momentum would be zero. This means that the angular momentum remains constant when a particle moves in a circular path about a fixed point. Thus, angular momentum is conserved.
solution_html:
Let the particle with velocity v be at point P at some instant t. We want to calculate the angular momentum of the particle about an arbitrary point O.

Let us consider that a particle of mass 'm' is moving around in a circular path about point O. The radius of the path is given by the radius vector 'r' and the linear velocity of the particle is 'v'. 
Its angular momentum is given as
L = r {tex}\times{/tex} mv
or L = r {tex}\times{/tex} p
Now, we can differentiate the above equation, thus
{tex}\frac {dL}{dt} = \frac {d}{dt} (r \times p){/tex}
or {tex}\frac {dL}{dt} = r \frac {dp}{dt} + p\frac {dr}{dt}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac {dL}{dt} = (r \times F) + p \times v{/tex}
Now, the product of p and v will be zero as these vectors are parallel (in the same direction) to each other.
thus,
{tex}\frac {dL}{dt} = (r \times F) = T{/tex}
So, the rate of change of angular momentum would be equal to the torque.
Here, as the system is free from outside influence, no external torque applied to the system. This means that
τ = 0
or {tex}\frac {dL}{dt}{/tex} = τ = 0
the rate of change of angular momentum would be zero. 
This means that the angular momentum remains constant when a particle moves in a circular path about a fixed point.
Thus, angular momentum is conserved.

question_text: Show that moment of a couple does not depend on the point about which you take the moments.
question_html: Show that moment of a couple does not depend on the point about which you take the moments.
solution_text: Consider a couple as shown in the figure, acting on a rigid body. The forces F and -F act respectively at points B and A. These points have position vectors r 1 and r 2 with respect to origin O. Let us take the moments of the forces about the origin. The moment of the couple = sum of the moments of the two forces making the couple {tex} = {r_1} \times ( - F) + {r_2} \times F{/tex} {tex} = {r_2} \times F - {r_1} \times F{/tex} {tex}({r_2} - {r_1}) \times F{/tex} But r 1 + AB = r 2 , and hence AB = r 2 - r 1 The moment of the couple, therefore, is AB {tex}\times{/tex} F It is clear from the above equation that moment of the couple is independent of the origin that is the point about which we have to take the moments of the forces.
solution_html:
Consider a couple as shown in the figure, acting on a rigid body. The forces F and -F act respectively at points B and A. These points have position vectors r1 and r2 with respect to origin O. Let us take the moments of the forces about the origin.
The moment of the couple = sum of the moments of the two forces making the couple
{tex} = {r_1} \times ( - F) + {r_2} \times F{/tex}
{tex} = {r_2} \times F - {r_1} \times F{/tex}
{tex}({r_2} - {r_1}) \times F{/tex}
But r1 + AB = r2, and hence AB = r2 - r1
The moment of the couple, therefore, is AB {tex}\times{/tex} F
It is clear from the above equation that moment of the couple is independent of the origin that is the point about which we have to take the moments of the forces.

question_text: A metal bar 70 cm long and 4.00 kg in mass supported on two knife-edges placed 10 cm from each end. A 6.00 kg load is suspended at 30 cm from one end. Find the reactions at the knife-edges. (Assume the bar to be of uniform cross-section and homogeneous).
question_html: A metal bar 70 cm long and 4.00 kg in mass supported on two knife-edges placed 10 cm from each end. A 6.00 kg load is suspended at 30 cm from one end. Find the reactions at the knife-edges. (Assume the bar to be of uniform cross-section and homogeneous).
solution_text: The figure shows the rod AB, the positions of the knife edges K 1 and K 2 , the centre of gravity of the rod at G, and the suspended load at P. Note the weight of the rod W acts at its centre of gravity G. The rod is uniform in cross-section and homogeneous; hence G is at the centre of the rod; AB = 70 cm. AG = 35 cm, AP = 30 cm, PG = 5 cm, AK 1 = BK 2 = 10 cm and K 1 G = K 2 G = 25 cm. Also, W = weight of the rod = 4.00 kg and W 1 = suspended load = 6.00 kg; R 1 and R 2 are the normal reactions of the support at the knife edges. For translational equilibrium of the rod, R 1 + R 2 - W 1 - W = 0 ..... (i) Note W 1 and W act vertically down and R 1 and R 2 act vertically up. For considering rotational equilibrium, we take moments of the forces. A convenient point to take moments about is G. The moments of R 2 and W 1 are anticlockwise (+ve), whereas the moment of R 1 is clockwise (-ve). For rotational equilibrium, -R 1 (K 1 G) + W 1 (PG) + R 2 (K 2 G) = 0 .... (ii) It is given that W = 4.00g N and W 1 = 6.00g N, where g = acceleration due to gravity. We take g = 9.8 m/s 2 . With numerical values inserted, from (i) R 1 + R 2 - 4.00g – 6.00g = 0 or R 1 + R 2 = 10.00g N ....(iii) = 98.00 N From (ii), -0.25 R 1 + 0.05 W 1 + 0.25 R 2 = 0 or R 1 - R 2 = 1.2g N = 11.76 N ....(iv) From (iii) and (iv), R 1 = 54.88 N, R 2 = 43.12 N Thus the reactions of the support are about 55 N at K 1 and 43 N at K 2 .

AG = 35 cm, AP = 30 cm, PG = 5 cm, AK1 = BK2 = 10 cm and K1G = K2G = 25 cm. Also, W = weight of the rod = 4.00 kg and W1= suspended load = 6.00 kg;
R1 and R2 are the normal reactions of the support at the knife edges.
For translational equilibrium of the rod,
R1 + R2 - W1 - W = 0 ..... (i)
Note W1 and W act vertically down and R1 and R2 act vertically up.
For considering rotational equilibrium, we take moments of the forces. A convenient point to take moments about is G. The moments of R2 and W1 are anticlockwise (+ve), whereas the moment of R1 is clockwise (-ve).
For rotational equilibrium,
-R1(K1G) + W1(PG) + R2(K2G) = 0 .... (ii)
It is given that W = 4.00g N and W1 = 6.00g
N, where g = acceleration due to gravity. We take g = 9.8 m/s2.
With numerical values inserted, from (i)
R1 + R2 - 4.00g – 6.00g = 0

question_text: A 3m long ladder weighing 20 kg leans on a frictionless wall. Its feet rest on the floor 1 m from the wall as shown in Fig. Find the reaction forces of the wall and the floor.
question_html: A 3m long ladder weighing 20 kg leans on a frictionless wall. Its feet rest on the floor 1 m from the wall as shown in Fig. Find the reaction forces of the wall and the floor.
solution_text: We have given that Length of the ladder, say AB = 3m Weight of ladder = 20Kg Distance of ladder from the wall, say AE = 1m From Pythagoras theorem, BC {tex}=2 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{m}{/tex} . The forces on the ladder are its weight W acting at its centre of gravity D, reaction forces F 1 and F 2 of the wall and the floor respectively. Force F 1 is perpendicular to the wall since the wall is frictionless. Force F 2 is resolved into two components, the normal reaction N and the force of friction F. Note that F prevents the ladder from sliding away from the wall and is therefore directed toward the wall. For translational equilibrium, taking the forces in the vertical direction, N - W = 0 ...(i) Taking the forces in the horizontal direction, F - F 1 = 0 ...(ii) For the rotational equilibrium, taking the moments of the forces about A, {tex}2 \sqrt{2} F_{1}-(1 / 2) W=0{/tex} ...(iii) Now W = 20g = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8N = 196.0 N From (i) N = 196.0 N From (iii) {tex}F_{1}=\frac {W} {4 \sqrt 2}= \frac {196.0} {4 \sqrt2}{/tex} = 34.6 N From (ii) F = 1 = 34.6 N {tex}F_{2}=\sqrt{F^{2}+N^{2}}=199.0 \mathrm{N}{/tex} The force F 2 makes an angle {tex}\alpha{/tex} with the horizontal, {tex}tan \ \alpha = \frac {N}{F} {/tex} , {tex}\alpha=\tan ^{-1}(4 \sqrt{2}) \approx 80^{\circ}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have given that 
Length of the ladder, say AB = 3m
 Weight of ladder = 20Kg
Distance of ladder from the wall, say AE = 1m
From Pythagoras theorem, BC {tex}=2 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{m}{/tex}. The forces on the ladder are its weight W acting at its centre of gravity D, reaction forces F1 and F2 of the wall and the floor respectively. Force F1 is perpendicular to the wall since the wall is frictionless. Force F2 is resolved into two components, the normal reaction N and the force of friction F.
Note that F prevents the ladder from sliding away from the wall and is therefore directed toward the wall.
For translational equilibrium, taking the forces in the vertical direction,
N - W = 0 ...(i)
Taking the forces in the horizontal direction,
F - F1 = 0 ...(ii)
For the rotational equilibrium, taking the moments of the forces about A,
{tex}2 \sqrt{2} F_{1}-(1 / 2) W=0{/tex} ...(iii)
Now W = 20g = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8N = 196.0 N
From (i) N = 196.0 N
From (iii) {tex}F_{1}=\frac {W} {4 \sqrt 2}= \frac {196.0} {4 \sqrt2}{/tex} = 34.6 N
From (ii) F = 1 = 34.6 N
{tex}F_{2}=\sqrt{F^{2}+N^{2}}=199.0 \mathrm{N}{/tex}
The force F2 makes an angle {tex}\alpha{/tex} with the horizontal,
{tex}tan \ \alpha = \frac {N}{F} {/tex}, {tex}\alpha=\tan ^{-1}(4 \sqrt{2}) \approx 80^{\circ}{/tex}

question_text: Obtain equation {tex}\omega=\omega_{0}+\alpha t{/tex} from first principles.
question_html: Obtain equation {tex}\omega=\omega_{0}+\alpha t{/tex} from first principles.
solution_text: The angular acceleration is uniform, Hence, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \omega}{\mathrm{d} t}=\alpha=\mathrm{constant}{/tex} ...(i) Integrating this equation, {tex}\omega=\int \alpha \mathrm{d} t+c{/tex} {tex}=\alpha t+c{/tex} (as {tex}\alpha{/tex} is constant) At t = 0, {tex}\omega=\omega_{0}{/tex} (given) From (i) we get at t = 0, {tex}\omega=c=\omega_{0}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\omega=\alpha t+\omega_{0}{/tex} as required. With the definition of {tex}\omega=\frac {\mathrm{d} \theta }{\mathrm{d} t}{/tex} we may integrate eq. {tex}\omega=\omega_{0}+\alpha t{/tex} to get eq. {tex}\theta=\theta_{0}+\omega_{0} t+\frac{1}{2} \alpha t^{2}{/tex} . This derivation and the derivation of Eq. {tex}\omega^{2}=\omega_{0}^{2}+2 \alpha\left(\theta-\theta_{0}\right){/tex} is left as an exercise.
solution_html:
The angular acceleration is uniform,
Hence,
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \omega}{\mathrm{d} t}=\alpha=\mathrm{constant}{/tex} ...(i)
Integrating this equation,
{tex}\omega=\int \alpha \mathrm{d} t+c{/tex}
{tex}=\alpha t+c{/tex} (as {tex}\alpha{/tex} is constant)
At t = 0, {tex}\omega=\omega_{0}{/tex} (given)
From (i) we get at t = 0, {tex}\omega=c=\omega_{0}{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\omega=\alpha t+\omega_{0}{/tex} as required.
With the definition of {tex}\omega=\frac {\mathrm{d} \theta }{\mathrm{d} t}{/tex}
we may integrate eq. {tex}\omega=\omega_{0}+\alpha t{/tex} to get eq. {tex}\theta=\theta_{0}+\omega_{0} t+\frac{1}{2} \alpha t^{2}{/tex}.
This derivation and the derivation of Eq. {tex}\omega^{2}=\omega_{0}^{2}+2 \alpha\left(\theta-\theta_{0}\right){/tex} is left as an exercise.

question_text: The angular speed of a motor wheel is increased from 1200 rpm to 3120 rpm in 16s. What is its angular acceleration, assuming the acceleration to be uniform? How many revolutions does the engine make during this time?
question_html:
The angular speed of a motor wheel is increased from 1200 rpm to 3120 rpm in 16s.

  1. What is its angular acceleration, assuming the acceleration to be uniform?
  2. How many revolutions does the engine make during this time?
solution_text: Given Initial revolution of the wheel is given by, {tex}N_0 = 1200 \;rpm{/tex} Final revolution of the wheel is given by, {tex}N = 3120\; rpm{/tex} Time, {tex}t = 16 s{/tex} Angular acceleration, {tex}\alpha =?{/tex} Initial angular speed is given by, {tex}\omega_0{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi N_{0}}{60}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi \times 1200}{60}{/tex} {tex}= 40\pi\ rad/s{/tex} Final angular speed is given by, {tex}\omega{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi N}{60}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 \pi \times 3120}{60}{/tex} {tex}= 104 \pi\ rad/s{/tex} Angular acceleration is, {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\omega-\omega_{0}}{t}{/tex} {tex}\alpha ={/tex} {tex}\frac{104 \pi-40 \pi}{16}{/tex} {tex}= 4\pi\ rad/s^2{/tex} Due to the angular displacement of the motor wheel, the number of revolutions of the wheel of the engine is {tex}N_E ={/tex} {tex}\frac{\theta}{2 \pi}{/tex} The angular displacement in time t is given by {tex}\theta = \omega_0t + \frac{1}{2}\alpha \ t^2{/tex} {tex}\theta = 40\pi \times 16 +\frac{1}{2} \times 4\pi \times (16)^2 = 1152\pi{/tex} rad The number of revolutions of the wheel of the engine is, {tex}N_E ={/tex} {tex}\frac{\theta}{2 \pi}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1152 \pi}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}N_E = 576{/tex}

    {tex}= 40\pi\ rad/s{/tex}
    Final angular speed is given by, {tex}\omega{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi N}{60}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 \pi \times 3120}{60}{/tex}{tex}= 104 \pi\  rad/s{/tex}
    Angular acceleration is, {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\omega-\omega_{0}}{t}{/tex}

solution_text: Force = 25N , hence Torque, {tex}\tau{/tex} {tex}= FR = 25\times 0.20 = 5 Nm{/tex} [ {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}R = 20 cm = 0.2 m{/tex} ] Moment of inertia is given by , I = {tex}\frac{M R^{2}}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{20 \times(0.2)^{2}}{2}{/tex} = 0.4kg - m 2 Angular acceleration, {tex}\alpha=\frac{\tau}{I}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{0.4}{/tex} {tex}= 12.5 rad/s^2{/tex} Work done by the pull, {tex}w = F\times s = 25\times2 = 50 J{/tex} KE = {tex}\frac{1}{2}I\omega^2{/tex} {tex}\omega^2 = \omega^{2}_{0}{/tex} + 2 {tex}\alpha \ \theta{/tex} {tex}= 0 + 2 \times 12.5 \times 10{/tex} [ {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\omega_{0} = 0, \theta = \frac{2}{0.2}=10{/tex} rad] {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}KE ={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} {tex}0.4 \times 250 = 50 J{/tex} From parts (ii) and (iii), {tex}KE = W{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} No loss of energy due to friction.

question_text: Give the location of the centre of mass of a sphere, cylinder, ring, and cube, each of uniform mass density. Does the centre of mass of a body necessarily lie inside the body?
question_html:
Give the location of the centre of mass of a

  1. sphere,
  2. cylinder,
  3. ring, and
  4. cube, each of uniform mass density.

Does the centre of mass of a body necessarily lie inside the body?
solution_text: The centre of mass (C.M.) is a point where the mass of the whole body is supposed to be concentrated. For the given geometric shapes having a uniform mass density, the C.M. lies at their respective geometric centers. geometrical centre the midpoint of its axis of symmetry geometrical centre at the point of intersection of its diagonals The centre of mass of a body need not necessarily lie within it. For example, the Center of the mass of bodies such as a ring, a hollow sphere, etc., lies outside the body.
solution_html:
The centre of mass (C.M.) is a point where the mass of the whole body is supposed to be concentrated. For the given geometric shapes having a uniform mass density, the C.M. lies at their respective geometric centers.

  1. geometrical centre
  2. the midpoint of its axis of symmetry
  3. geometrical centre
  4. at the point of intersection of its diagonals

The centre of mass of a body need not necessarily lie within it. For example, the Center of the mass of bodies such as a ring, a hollow sphere, etc., lies outside the body.

question_text: In the HCl molecule, the separation between the nuclei of the two atoms is about 1.27 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} \left( {{\text{1}}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {\text{ = 1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 10}}}}{\text{m}}} \right){/tex} . Find the approximate location of the C.M of the molecule, given that a chlorine atom is about 35.5 times as massive as a hydrogen atom and nearly all the mass of an atom is concentrated in its nucleus.
question_html: In the HCl molecule, the separation between the nuclei of the two atoms is about 1.27 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} \left( {{\text{1}}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {\text{ = 1}}{{\text{0}}^{{\text{ - 10}}}}{\text{m}}} \right){/tex}. Find the approximate location of the C.M of the molecule, given that a chlorine atom is about 35.5 times as massive as a hydrogen atom and nearly all the mass of an atom is concentrated in its nucleus.
solution_text: The centre of mass is a position defined relative to an object or system of objects. It is the average position of all the parts of the system, weighted according to their masses. The given situation can be shown as: Distance between H and Cl atoms = 1.27 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} Mass of H atom = m Mass of Cl atom = 35.5 m Let the centre of mass of the system lie at a distance x from the Cl atom. Distance of the centre of mass from the H atom = (1.27 - x) Let us assume that the centre of mass of the given molecule lies at the origin. Therefore, we can have: {tex}\frac { m ( 1.27 - x ) + 35.5 m x } { m + 35.5 m } = 0{/tex} m (1.27 - x) + 35.5 mx = 0 1.27 - x = -35.5 mx {tex}\therefore x = \frac { - 1.27 } { ( 35.5 - 1 ) } = - 0.037 \mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} Here, the negative sign indicates that the centre of mass lies at the left of the Cl atom.
solution_html:
The centre of mass is a position defined relative to an object or system of objects. It is the average position of all the parts of the system, weighted according to their masses. The given situation can be shown as:

Distance between H and Cl atoms = 1.27{tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex}
Mass of H atom = m
Mass of Cl atom = 35.5 m
Let the centre of mass of the system lie at a distance x from the Cl atom.
Distance of the centre of mass from the H atom = (1.27 - x)
Let us assume that the centre of mass of the given molecule lies at the origin. Therefore, we can have:
{tex}\frac { m ( 1.27 - x ) + 35.5 m x } { m + 35.5 m } = 0{/tex}
m (1.27 - x) + 35.5 mx = 0
1.27 - x = -35.5 mx
{tex}\therefore x = \frac { - 1.27 } { ( 35.5 - 1 ) } = - 0.037 \mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex}
Here, the negative sign indicates that the centre of mass lies at the left of the Cl atom.

question_text: A child sits stationary at one end of a long trolley moving uniformly with a speed V on a smooth horizontal floor. If the child gets up and runs about on the trolley in any manner, what is the speed of the CM of the (trolley + child) system?
question_html: A child sits stationary at one end of a long trolley moving uniformly with a speed V on a smooth horizontal floor. If the child gets up and runs about on the trolley in any manner, what is the speed of the CM of the (trolley + child) system?
solution_text: No change. The child is running arbitrarily on a trolley moving with velocity v. However, the running of the child will produce no effect on the velocity of the centre of mass of the trolley. Here, the boy running on the trolley(either forward or backward) will exert an only internal force on running trolley. Only external forces can affect the centre motion of the body. This is because the force due to the boy's motion is purely internal. Internal forces produce no effect on the motion of the bodies on which they act. Since no external force is involved in the boy-trolley system, the boy's motion will produce no change in the velocity of the centre of mass of the trolley.
solution_html:
No change.
The child is running arbitrarily on a trolley moving with velocity v. However, the running of the child will produce no effect on the velocity of the centre of mass of the trolley. Here, the boy running on the trolley(either forward or backward) will exert an only internal force on running trolley. Only external forces can affect the centre motion of the body. This is because the force due to the boy's motion is purely internal. Internal forces produce no effect on the motion of the bodies on which they act. Since no external force is involved in the boy-trolley system, the boy's motion will produce no change in the velocity of the centre of mass of the trolley.

solution_text: To calculate the area of the parallogram using vector method ,we will proceed in the following manner. Above problem can be Proved in such manner.

question_text: Show that a.(b {tex}\times{/tex} c) is equal in magnitude to the volume of the parallelepiped formed on the three vectors, a, b and c .
question_html: Show that a.(b {tex}\times{/tex} c) is equal in magnitude to the volume of the parallelepiped formed on the three vectors, a, b and c.
solution_text: In geometry, a parallelepiped is a three-dimensional figure formed by six parallelograms (the term rhomboid is also sometimes used with this meaning). By analogy, it relates to a parallelogram just as a cube relates to a square or as a cuboid to a rectangle. A parallelepiped with origin O and sides a, b, and c is shown in the following figure for which we have to find the enclosed volume - Volume of the given parallelepiped = abc {tex}\overline { O A } = \vec { a }{/tex} {tex}\overline { O B } = \vec { b }{/tex} {tex}\overline { O C } = \vec { c }{/tex} Let {tex}\hat { n }{/tex} be a unit vector perpendicular to both b and c. Hence, {tex}\hat { n }{/tex} and a have the same direction as a is perpendicular to both b and c. {tex}\therefore \vec { b } \times \vec { c } = b c \sin \theta \hat { n }{/tex} {tex} = b c \sin 90 ^ { \circ } \hat { n }{/tex} {tex}= b c \hat { n }{/tex} {tex}\vec { a } \cdot ( \vec { b } \times \vec { c } ){/tex} {tex}= a \cdot ( b c \hat { n } ){/tex} {tex}= abc \;cos \theta \hat { n }{/tex} = abc cos 0°(as angle between n and a will be 0 0 or 180 0 ) = abc Thus, Volume of the parallelepiped is equal to multiplication of a,b and c and can be proved as above.

question_text: Find the components along the x, y, z axes of the angular momentum l of a particle, whose position vector is r with components x, y, z and momentum is p with components p x , p y and p z . Show that if the particle moves only in the x-y plane the angular momentum has only a z-component.
question_html: Find the components along the x, y, z axes of the angular momentum l of a particle, whose position vector is r with components x, y, z and momentum is p with components px, py and pz. Show that if the particle moves only in the x-y plane the angular momentum has only a z-component.
solution_text: l x = yp z - zp y l y = zp x - xp z l z = xp y - yp x The linear momentum of the particle in cartesian coordinate, {tex}\vec { p } = p _ { x } \hat { i } + p _ { y } \hat { j } + p _ { z } \hat { k }{/tex} Position vector of the particle in cartesian coordiantes , {tex}\vec { r } = x \hat { i } + \hat { y } + z \hat { k }{/tex} As we know the angular momentum of a moving particle about a point is given as, {tex}\vec { l } = \vec { r } \times \vec { p }{/tex} where p and r are linear momentum and position vector respectively, {tex}= \left( x \hat { i } + \hat { y } ^ { \prime } + z \hat { k } \right) \times \left( p _ { x } \hat { i } + p _ { y } \hat { j } + p _ { z } \hat { k } \right){/tex} {tex}= \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { x } & { y } & { z } \\ { p _ { x } } & { p _ { y } } & { p _ { z } } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}l _ { x } \hat { i } + l _ { y } \hat { j } + l _ { z } \hat { k } {/tex} {tex}= \hat { i } \left( y p _ { z } - z p _ { y } \right) - \hat { j } \left( x p _ { z } - z p _ { x } \right) + \hat { k } \left( x p _ { y } - y p _ { x } \right){/tex} = {tex}= \hat { i } \left( y p _ { z } - z p _ { y } \right) +\hat { j } \left(- x p _ { z } +z p _ { x } \right) + \hat { k } \left( x p _ { y } - y p _ { x } \right){/tex} Comparing the coefficients of {tex}\hat { i } , \hat { j } , \text { and } \hat { k }{/tex} we get the components of angular momentum as : l x = y p z - zp y l y = xp z - zp x …..(i) l z = xp y - yp x b) If the particle moves in the x-y plane only. Hence, the z-component of the position vector and z component of linear momentum vector become zero, i.e., z = p z = 0 Thus, equation (i) reduces to: l x = 0 l y = 0 l z = xp y - yp x Therefore, when the particle is confined to move in the x-y plane, the x and y components of linear momentum are zero and hence the direction of angular momentum is along the z-direction.

 As we know the angular momentum of a moving particle about a point is given as, {tex}\vec { l } = \vec { r } \times \vec { p }{/tex} where p and r are linear momentum and position vector respectively,

{tex}= \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { x } & { y } & { z } \\ { p _ { x } } & { p _ { y } } & { p _ { z } } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex}l _ { x } \hat { i } + l _ { y } \hat { j } + l _ { z } \hat { k } {/tex} {tex}= \hat { i } \left( y p _ { z } - z p _ { y } \right) - \hat { j } \left( x p _ { z } - z p _ { x } \right) + \hat { k } \left( x p _ { y } - y p _ { x } \right){/tex}

Comparing the coefficients of {tex}\hat { i } , \hat { j } , \text { and } \hat { k }{/tex} we get the components of angular momentum as :
lx = ypz - zpy

b)  If the particle moves in the x-y plane only. Hence, the z-component of the position vector and z component of linear momentum vector become zero, i.e.,

question_text: Two particles, each of mass m and speed v, travel in opposite directions along parallel lines separated by a distance d. Show that the vector angular momentum of the two particle system is the same whatever be the point about which the angular momentum is taken.
question_html: Two particles, each of mass m and speed v, travel in opposite directions along parallel lines separated by a distance d. Show that the vector angular momentum of the two particle system is the same whatever be the point about which the angular momentum is taken.
solution_text: Let at a certain instant two particles be at points P and Q between two parallel lines and d is the perpendicular distance between them, as shown in the following figure. The angular momentum of the system about point P: {tex}\vec { L } _ { p } = m v \times 0 + m v \times d{/tex} , = mvd ….(i) The angular momentum of the system about point Q: {tex}\vec { L } _ { Q } = m v \times d + m v \times 0{/tex} = mvd ….(ii) Consider a point R, which is at a distance y from point Q, i.e., QR = y {tex}\therefore{/tex} PR = d - y The angular momentum of the system about point R: {tex}\vec { L } _ { R } = m v \times ( d - y ) + m v \times y{/tex} = mvd - mvy + mvy = mvd …(iii) Comparing equations (i), (ii), and (iii), we get: {tex}\vec { L } _ { p } = \vec { L } _ { Q } = \vec { L } _ { R }{/tex} …(iv) We infer from equation (iv) that the angular momentum of a system does not depend on the point about which it is taken. So the total angular momentum is expressed as L = mvd.
solution_html:
Let at a certain instant two particles be at points P and Q between two parallel lines and d is the perpendicular distance between them, as shown in the following figure.

The angular momentum of the system about point P:
{tex}\vec { L } _ { p } = m v \times 0 + m v \times d{/tex},
= mvd ….(i)
The angular momentum of the system about point Q:
{tex}\vec { L } _ { Q } = m v \times d + m v \times 0{/tex}
= mvd ….(ii)
Consider a point R, which is at a distance y from point Q, i.e.,
QR = y
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PR = d - y
The angular momentum of the system about point R:
{tex}\vec { L } _ { R } = m v \times ( d - y ) + m v \times y{/tex}
= mvd - mvy + mvy
= mvd …(iii)
Comparing equations (i), (ii), and (iii), we get:
{tex}\vec { L } _ { p } = \vec { L } _ { Q } = \vec { L } _ { R }{/tex} …(iv)
We infer from equation (iv) that the angular momentum of a system does not depend on the point about which it is taken. So the total angular momentum is expressed as L = mvd.

question_html: A non-uniform bar of weight W is suspended at rest by two strings of negligible weight as shown in fig. The angles made by the strings with the vertical are 36.9o and 53.1o respectively. The bar is 2 m long. Calculate the distance d of the centre of gravity of the bar from its left end.
solution_text: Given, Length of the bar, l = 2 m Let T 1 and T 2 be the tensions produced by the strings.ṁ The free body diagram can be drawn as, For transitional equilibrium we have,ṁ T 1 sin36.9 = T 2 sin 53.1 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}} = \frac{{\sin 53.1}}{{\sin 36.9}} = \frac{4}{3}{/tex} ṁ {tex}\therefore {T_1} = \frac{4}{3}{T_2}{/tex} For rotational equilibrium, on taking the torque about the centre of gravity. We have, {tex}{T_1}\left( {\cos 36.9} \right) \times d = {T_2}\left( {\cos 53.1} \right) \times \left( {2 - d} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} T 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} d = T 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} (2 - d) {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{4}{3}{T_2} \times 0.8 \times d = {T_2}\left[ {(0.6 \times 2) - (0.6 \times d)} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1.067d + 0.6d = 1.2 {tex}\therefore d = \frac{{1.2}}{{1.67}} = 0.72m{/tex} Hence, Centre of gravity lies at a distance of 0.72 m from the left side of the bar.

question_text: A car weighs 1800 kg. The distance between its front and back axles is 1.8 m. Its centre of gravity is 1.05 m behind the front axle. Determine the force exerted by the level ground on each front wheel and each back wheel.
question_html: A car weighs 1800 kg. The distance between its front and back axles is 1.8 m. Its centre of gravity is 1.05 m behind the front axle. Determine the force exerted by the level ground on each front wheel and each back wheel.
solution_text: Weight of car = 1800 Kg Distance of COG from front axle = 1.05 m Distance of COG from back axle = 1.8 - 1.05 = 0.75 m Vertical forces are balanced , So,At translational equilibrium: R 1 + R 2 = mg R 1 + R 2 = 1800 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 = 17640 R 1 and R 2 are the forces exerted by the level ground on the front and back wheels respectively. Angular momentum about centre of gravity is zero. So, R 1 (1.05) = R 2 (1.8 - 1.05) {tex}R _ { 1 } \times 1.05 = R _ { 2} \times 0.75{/tex} {tex}\frac { R _ { 1 } } { R _ { 2 } } = \frac { 0.75 } { 1.05 } = \frac { 5 } { 7 }{/tex} {tex}\frac { R _ { 1 } } { R _ { 2 } } = \frac { 7 } { 5 }{/tex} R 1 = 1.4 R 2 …..(ii) Solving equations (i) and (ii), we get: 1.4 R 2 + R 2 = 17640 {tex}R _ { 2 } = \frac { 17640 } { 2.4 } = 7350 \mathrm { N }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} R 1 = 17640 - 7350 = 10290 N Therefore, the force exerted on each front wheel {tex}=\frac { R_1 } { 2 }= \frac { 7350 } { 2 } = 3675 N{/tex} , and The force exerted on each back wheel {tex}= \frac { R_2 } { 2 }=\frac { 10290 } { 2 } = 5145 N{/tex}
solution_html:
Weight of car = 1800 Kg
Distance of COG from front axle = 1.05 m 
Distance of COG from back axle = 1.8 - 1.05 = 0.75 m 
Vertical forces are balanced , 
So,At translational equilibrium:
R1 + R2 = mg 
R1 + R2 = 1800 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 = 17640

R1 and R2 are the forces exerted by the level ground on the front and back wheels respectively.
Angular momentum about centre of gravity is zero.
So, 
R1(1.05) = R2(1.8 - 1.05)
{tex}R _ { 1 } \times 1.05 = R _ { 2} \times 0.75{/tex}
{tex}\frac { R _ { 1 } } { R _ { 2 } } = \frac { 0.75 } { 1.05 } = \frac { 5 } { 7 }{/tex}
{tex}\frac { R _ { 1 } } { R _ { 2 } } = \frac { 7 } { 5 }{/tex}
R1 = 1.4 R2 …..(ii)
Solving equations (i) and (ii), we get:
1.4 R2 + R2 = 17640
{tex}R _ { 2 } = \frac { 17640 } { 2.4 } = 7350 \mathrm { N }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} R1 = 17640 - 7350 = 10290 N
Therefore, the force exerted on each front wheel {tex}=\frac { R_1 } { 2 }= \frac { 7350 } { 2 } = 3675 N{/tex}, and
The force exerted on each back wheel {tex}= \frac { R_2 } { 2 }=\frac { 10290 } { 2 } = 5145 N{/tex}

question_text: Torques of equal magnitude is applied to a hollow cylinder and a solid sphere, both having the same mass and radius. The cylinder is free to rotate about its standard axis of symmetry, and the sphere is free to rotate about an axis passing through its centre. Which of the two will acquire a greater angular speed after a given time?
question_html: Torques of equal magnitude is applied to a hollow cylinder and a solid sphere, both having the same mass and radius. The cylinder is free to rotate about its standard axis of symmetry, and the sphere is free to rotate about an axis passing through its centre. Which of the two will acquire a greater angular speed after a given time?
solution_text: Let m and r be the respective masses of the hollow cylinder and the solid sphere. moment of inertia of the hollow cylinder I 1 = mr 2 The moment of inertia of the solid sphere I 2 {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 5 } m r ^ { 2 }{/tex} We have the relation: {tex}\tau = I a{/tex} For the hollow cylinder, {tex}\tau _ { 1 } = I _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 1 }{/tex} For the solid sphere, {tex}\tau _ { 2 } = I _ { 2 } \alpha _ { 2 }{/tex} As an equal torque is applied to both the bodies, {tex}\tau _ { 1 } = \tau _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac {\alpha_ { 2 } } { \alpha _ { 1 } } = \frac { I _ { 1} } { I _ { 2 } } = \frac { M r ^ { 2 } } { \frac { 2 } { 5 } M r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 2 } { 5 }{/tex} {tex}a _ { 2 } > a _ { 1 }{/tex} ….(i) Now, using the relation: {tex}\omega = \omega _ { 0 } + a t{/tex} {tex}\omega \propto a{/tex} …(ii) From equations (i) and (ii), we can write: {tex}\omega _ { 2 } > \omega _ { 1 }{/tex} Hence, the angular velocity of the solid sphere will be greater than that of the hollow cylinder.
solution_html:
Let m and r be the respective masses of the hollow cylinder and the solid sphere.
 moment of inertia of the hollow cylinder I1 = mr2
The moment of inertia of the solid sphere I2 {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 5 } m r ^ { 2 }{/tex}
We have the relation:
{tex}\tau = I a{/tex}
For the hollow cylinder, {tex}\tau _ { 1 } = I _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 1 }{/tex}
For the solid sphere, {tex}\tau _ { 2 } = I _ { 2 } \alpha _ { 2 }{/tex}
As an equal torque is applied to both the bodies, {tex}\tau _ { 1 } = \tau _ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac {\alpha_ { 2 } } { \alpha _ { 1 } } = \frac { I _ { 1} } { I _ { 2 } } = \frac { M r ^ { 2 } } { \frac { 2 } { 5 } M r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 2 } { 5 }{/tex}
{tex}a _ { 2 } > a _ { 1 }{/tex} ….(i)
Now, using the relation:
{tex}\omega = \omega _ { 0 } + a t{/tex}
{tex}\omega \propto a{/tex} …(ii)
From equations (i) and (ii), we can write:
{tex}\omega _ { 2 } > \omega _ { 1 }{/tex}
Hence, the angular velocity of the solid sphere will be greater than that of the hollow cylinder.

question_text: A solid cylinder of mass 20 kg rotates about its axis with angular speed 100 rad s -1 . The radius of the cylinder is 0.25 m. What is the kinetic energy associated with the rotation of the cylinder? What is the magnitude of angular momentum of the cylinder about its axis?
question_html: A solid cylinder of mass 20 kg rotates about its axis with angular speed 100 rad s-1. The radius of the cylinder is 0.25 m. What is the kinetic energy associated with the rotation of the cylinder? What is the magnitude of angular momentum of the cylinder about its axis?
solution_text: Mass of the cylinder, m = 20 kg Angular speed, {tex}\omega {/tex} = 100 rad s -1 Radius of the cylinder, r = 0.25 m The moment of inertia of the solid cylinder: {tex}I = \frac { m r ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 20 \times ( 0.25 ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} = 0.625 kg m 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Kinetic energy {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } I \omega ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 6.25 \times ( 100 ) ^ { 2 } = 3125 J{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Angular momentum, L= I {tex}\omega{/tex} = 0.625 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 62.5 Js
solution_html:
Mass of the cylinder, m = 20 kg
Angular speed, {tex}\omega {/tex} = 100 rad s-1
Radius of the cylinder, r = 0.25 m
The moment of inertia of the solid cylinder:
{tex}I = \frac { m r ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 20 \times ( 0.25 ) ^ { 2 }{/tex}
= 0.625 kg m2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Kinetic energy {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } I \omega ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 6.25 \times ( 100 ) ^ { 2 } = 3125 J{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Angular momentum, L= I{tex}\omega{/tex}
= 0.625 {tex}\times{/tex} 100
= 62.5 Js

question_text: A child stands at the centre of a turntable with his two arms outstretched. The turntable is set rotating with an angular speed of 40 rev/min. How much is the angular speed of the child if he folds his hands back and thereby reduces his moment of inertia to 2/5 times the initial value? Assume that the turntable rotates without friction. Show that the child's new kinetic energy of rotation is more than the initial kinetic energy of rotation. How do you account for this increase in kinetic energy?
question_html:
1. A child stands at the centre of a turntable with his two arms outstretched. The turntable is set rotating with an angular speed of 40 rev/min. How much is the angular speed of the child if he folds his hands back and thereby reduces his moment of inertia to 2/5 times the initial value? Assume that the turntable rotates without friction.
  2. Show that the child's new kinetic energy of rotation is more than the initial kinetic energy of rotation. How do you account for this increase in kinetic energy?
solution_text: Initial angular velocity, {tex}\omega _ 1{/tex} = 40 rev/min Final angular velocity = {tex}\omega _ { 2 }{/tex} The moment of inertia of the boy with stretched hands = I 1 The moment of inertia of the boy with folded hands = I 2 The two moments of inertia are related as: {tex}I _ { 2 } = \frac { 2 } { 5 } I _ { 1 }{/tex} Since no external force and hence external torque acts on the boy, the angular momentum L of the boy is a constant. Hence, for the two situations, we can write: {tex}I _ { 2 } \omega _ { 2 } = I _ { 1 } \omega _ { 1 }{/tex} ( angular momentum L=Iw) {tex}\omega _ { 2 } = \frac { I _ { 1 } } { I _ { 2 } } \omega _ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { I _ { 1 } } { \frac { 2 } { 5 } I _ { 1 } } \times 40 = \frac { 5 } { 2 } \times 40{/tex} = 100 rev/min The final kinetic energy of rotation, {tex}E _ { F } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } I _ { 2 } \omega _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} The initial kinetic energy of rotation, {tex}E _ { I } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } I _ { 1 } \omega _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\frac { E _ { F } } { E _ { I } } = \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } I _ { 2 } \omega _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } I _ { 1 } \omega _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 I _ { 1 } ( 100 ) ^ { 2 } } { 5 I _ { 1 } ( 40 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} (given {tex}I _ { 2 } = \frac { 2 } { 5 } I _ { 1 }{/tex} ) {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 5 } \times \frac { 100 \times 100 } { 40 \times 40 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 5 } { 2 } = 2.5{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} E F = 2.5 E 1 Hence, it is clear that final kinetic energy of rotation is more than initial kinetic energy of rotation.
solution_html:
1. Initial angular velocity, {tex}\omega _ 1{/tex} = 40 rev/min
    Final angular velocity = {tex}\omega _ { 2 }{/tex}
    The moment of inertia of the boy with stretched hands = I1
    The moment of inertia of the boy with folded hands = I2
    The two moments of inertia are related as:
    {tex}I _ { 2 } = \frac { 2 } { 5 } I _ { 1 }{/tex}
    Since no external force and hence external torque acts on the boy, the angular momentum L of the boy is a constant.
    Hence, for the two situations, we can write:
    {tex}I _ { 2 } \omega _ { 2 } = I _ { 1 } \omega _ { 1 }{/tex}    ( angular momentum L=Iw)
    {tex}\omega _ { 2 } = \frac { I _ { 1 } } { I _ { 2 } } \omega _ { 1 }{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { I _ { 1 } } { \frac { 2 } { 5 } I _ { 1 } } \times 40 = \frac { 5 } { 2 } \times 40{/tex}
    = 100 rev/min
  2.  The final kinetic energy of rotation, {tex}E _ { F } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } I _ { 2 } \omega _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    The initial kinetic energy of rotation, {tex}E _ { I } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } I _ { 1 } \omega _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    {tex}\frac { E _ { F } } { E _ { I } } = \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } I _ { 2 } \omega _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } I _ { 1 } \omega _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } }{/tex}       
    {tex}= \frac { 2 I _ { 1 } ( 100 ) ^ { 2 } } { 5 I _ { 1 } ( 40 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} (given {tex}I _ { 2 } = \frac { 2 } { 5 } I _ { 1 }{/tex} )
    {tex}= \frac { 2 } { 5 } \times \frac { 100 \times 100 } { 40 \times 40 }{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { 5 } { 2 } = 2.5{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {/tex}EF = 2.5 E1  Hence, it is clear that final kinetic energy of rotation is more than initial kinetic energy of rotation.

question_text: A rope of negligible mass is wound round a hollow cylinder of mass 3 kg and radius 40 cm. What is the angular acceleration of the cylinder if the rope is pulled with a force of 30 N? What is the linear acceleration of the rope? Assume that there is no slipping.
question_html: A rope of negligible mass is wound round a hollow cylinder of mass 3 kg and radius 40 cm. What is the angular acceleration of the cylinder if the rope is pulled with a force of 30 N? What is the linear acceleration of the rope? Assume that there is no slipping.
solution_text: Mass of the hollow cylinder, m = 3 kg Radius of the hollow cylinder, r = 40 cm = 0.4 m Applied force, F = 30 N The moment of inertia of the hollow cylinder about its geometric axis: I = mr 2 {tex}= 3 \times ( 0.4 ) ^ { 2 } = 0.48 k g m ^ { 2 }{/tex} Torque, {tex}\tau = F \times r{/tex} = 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.4 = 12 Nm For angular acceleration a, torque is also given by the relation: {tex}\tau = I a{/tex} {tex}a = \frac { \tau } { I } = \frac { 12 } { 0.48 }{/tex} = 25 rad s -2 Linear acceleration = rα = 0.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 25 = 10 m s -2
solution_html:
Mass of the hollow cylinder, m = 3 kg
Radius of the hollow cylinder, r = 40 cm = 0.4 m
Applied force, F = 30 N
The moment of inertia of the hollow cylinder about its geometric axis:
I = mr2
{tex}= 3 \times ( 0.4 ) ^ { 2 } = 0.48 k g m ^ { 2 }{/tex}
Torque, {tex}\tau = F \times r{/tex}
= 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.4 = 12 Nm
For angular acceleration a, torque is also given by the relation:
{tex}\tau = I a{/tex}
{tex}a = \frac { \tau } { I } = \frac { 12 } { 0.48 }{/tex}
= 25 rad s-2
Linear acceleration = rα = 0.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 25 = 10 m s-2

question_text: To maintain a rotor at a uniform angular speed of 200 rad s -1 , an engine needs to transmit a torque of 180 Nm. What is the power required by the engine? (Note: uniform angular velocity in the absence of friction implies zero torque. In practice, applied torque is needed to counter frictional torque). Assume that the engine is 100% efficient.
question_html: To maintain a rotor at a uniform angular speed of 200 rad s-1, an engine needs to transmit a torque of 180 Nm. What is the power required by the engine? (Note: uniform angular velocity in the absence of friction implies zero torque. In practice, applied torque is needed to counter frictional torque). Assume that the engine is 100% efficient.
solution_text: The power needed, {tex}P = \tau \times \omega {/tex} Where {tex}\tau{/tex} is torque needed to counter friction {tex}\omega {/tex} is angular speed to be maintained Given, The torque needed, {tex}\tau{/tex} = 180 N m Angular speed, {tex}\omega {/tex} = 200 rad s -1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} power required to maintain rotation of 200 rad s -1 by countering frictional torque of 180 N m, P = 180 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 = 36 kW
solution_html:
The power needed,
{tex}P = \tau \times \omega {/tex}
Where
{tex}\tau{/tex} is torque needed to counter friction
{tex}\omega {/tex} is angular speed to be maintained
Given,
The torque needed, {tex}\tau{/tex} = 180 N m
Angular speed, {tex}\omega {/tex} = 200 rad s-1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} power required to maintain rotation of 200 rad s-1 by countering frictional torque of 180 N m,
P = 180 {tex}\times{/tex} 200
= 36 kW

question_text: From a uniform disk of radius R, a circular hole of radius {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex} is cut out. The centre of the hole is at {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex} from the centre of the original disc. Locate the centre of gravity of the resulting flat body.
question_html: From a uniform disk of radius R, a circular hole of radius {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex} is cut out. The centre of the hole is at {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex} from the centre of the original disc. Locate the centre of gravity of the resulting flat body.
solution_text: The centre of mass of an object is the point at which the object can be balanced. Mathematically, it is the point at which the torques from the mass elements of an object sum to zero. The centre of mass is useful because problems can often be simplified by treating a collection of masses as one mass at their common centre of mass. The weight of the object then acts through this point. To solve this problem, first we assume that the whole disc was present whose centre of mass lies at the origin from which a small disc was cut out. So CM of remaining portion and cut out disc will lie exactly at the origin i.e Centre of Mass of the original disc at x = 0 Mass per unit area of the original disc = {tex}\sigma{/tex} Radius of the original disc = R Mass of the original disc, {tex}M = \pi R ^ { 2 } \sigma{/tex} The disc with the cut portion is shown in the following figure: Radius of the smaller disc {tex}= \frac { R } { 2 }{/tex} Mass of the smaller disc, {tex}M ^ { \prime } = \pi \left( \frac { R } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \sigma = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \pi R ^ { 2 } \sigma = \frac { M } { 4 }{/tex} Let O and O' be the respective centers of the original disc and the disc cut off from the original. As per the definition of the centre of mass, the centre of mass of the original disc is supposed to be concentrated at O, while that of the smaller disc is supposed to be concentrated at O'. It is given that: {tex}O O ^ { \prime } = { \frac { R } { 2 } }{/tex} After the smaller disc has been cut from the original, the remaining portion is considered to be a system of two masses. The two masses are: M (concentrated at O), and {tex}\left(-M' = \frac { M } { 4 } \right){/tex} concentrated at O' (The negative sign indicates that this portion has been removed from the original disc.) Let x be the distance through which the centre of mass of the remaining portion shifts from point O. The relation between the centers of masses of two masses is given as: {tex}x = \frac { m _ { 1 } r _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } }{/tex} For the given system {tex}x = \frac { M \times 0 - M \times \left( \frac { R } { 2 } \right) } { M + \left( - M ^ { \prime } \right) }{/tex} (here M' is M/4 ) {tex}= \frac { \frac { - M } { 4 } \times \frac { R } { 2 } } { M - \frac { M } { 4 } } = \frac { - M R } { 8 } \times \frac { 4 } { 3 M } = \frac { - R } { 6 }{/tex} Note that shift in Centre of Mass is very less(only 0.16 R or {tex}\frac{R}{6}{/tex} ) as removed portion has very less mass as compared to the remaining portion. (The negative sign indicates that the centre of mass gets shifted toward the left of point O and lies at {tex}\frac{R}{6}{/tex} left towards origin.)
solution_html:
The centre of mass of an object is the point at which the object can be balanced. Mathematically, it is the point at which the torques from the mass elements of an object sum to zero. The centre of mass is useful because problems can often be simplified by treating a collection of masses as one mass at their common centre of mass. The weight of the object then acts through this point.
To solve this problem, first we assume that the whole disc was present whose centre of mass lies at the origin from which a small disc was cut out. So CM of remaining portion and cut out disc will lie exactly at the origin i.e Centre of Mass of the original disc at x = 0
Mass per unit area of the original disc = {tex}\sigma{/tex}
Radius of the original disc = R
Mass of the original disc, {tex}M = \pi R ^ { 2 } \sigma{/tex}
The disc with the cut portion is shown in the following figure:

Radius of the smaller disc {tex}= \frac { R } { 2 }{/tex}
Mass of the smaller disc, {tex}M ^ { \prime } = \pi \left( \frac { R } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \sigma = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \pi R ^ { 2 } \sigma = \frac { M } { 4 }{/tex}
Let O and O' be the respective centers of the original disc and the disc cut off from the original. As per the definition of the centre of mass, the centre of mass of the original disc is supposed to be concentrated at O, while that of the smaller disc is supposed to be concentrated at O'.
It is given that:
{tex}O O ^ { \prime } = { \frac { R } { 2 } }{/tex}
After the smaller disc has been cut from the original, the remaining portion is considered to be a system of two masses. The two masses are:
M (concentrated at O), and
 {tex}\left(-M' = \frac { M } { 4 } \right){/tex} concentrated at O'
(The negative sign indicates that this portion has been removed from the original disc.)
Let x be the distance through which the centre of mass of the remaining portion shifts from point O.
The relation between the centers of masses of two masses is given as:
{tex}x = \frac { m _ { 1 } r _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } }{/tex}
For the given system
{tex}x = \frac { M \times 0 - M \times \left( \frac { R } { 2 } \right) } { M + \left( - M ^ { \prime } \right) }{/tex}    (here M' is M/4 )
{tex}= \frac { \frac { - M } { 4 } \times \frac { R } { 2 } } { M - \frac { M } { 4 } } = \frac { - M R } { 8 } \times \frac { 4 } { 3 M } = \frac { - R } { 6 }{/tex}

Note that shift in Centre of Mass is very less(only 0.16 R or {tex}\frac{R}{6}{/tex}) as removed portion has very less mass as compared to the remaining portion.
(The negative sign indicates that the centre of mass gets shifted toward the left of point O and lies at {tex}\frac{R}{6}{/tex} left towards origin.)

question_text: A metre stick is balanced on a knife edge at its centre. When two coins, each of mass 5 g are put one on top of the other at the 12.0 cm mark, the stick is found to be balanced at 45.0 cm. What is the mass of the metre stick?
question_html: A metre stick is balanced on a knife edge at its centre. When two coins, each of mass 5 g are put one on top of the other at the 12.0 cm mark, the stick is found to be balanced at 45.0 cm. What is the mass of the metre stick?
solution_text: Let W and W' be the respective weights of the meter stick and both the coins. The mass of the meter stick is concentrated at its mid-point, i.e., at the 50 cm mark. Let the mass of the meter stick = M Mass of each coin, m = 5 g When the coins are placed 12 cm away from the end P, due to the weights of the coin, the center of mass gets shifted by 5 cm from point R toward the end P. The center of mass is located at a distance of 45 cm from point P. According to the principle of moments, for equilibrium net torque on the system about the center of mass S should be zero. Hence -10 {tex}\times{/tex} g(45 - 12) + m'g(50 - 45) = 0 (Using the convention; clockwise torque = -ve and anticlockwise = +ve) hence the mass of the stick {tex}\therefore m ^ { \prime } = \frac { 10 \times 33 } { 5 } = 66 g{/tex} Hence, the mass of the metre stick is 66 g.
solution_html:
Let W and W' be the respective weights of the meter stick and both the coins.

The mass of the meter stick is concentrated at its mid-point, i.e., at the 50 cm mark.
Let the mass of the meter stick = M
Mass of each coin, m = 5 g
When the coins are placed 12 cm away from the end P, due to the weights of the coin, the center of mass gets shifted by 5 cm from point R toward the end P. The center of mass is located at a distance of 45 cm from point P.
According to the principle of moments, for equilibrium net torque on the system about the center of mass S should be zero. Hence
-10 {tex}\times{/tex} g(45 - 12) + m'g(50 - 45) = 0 (Using the convention; clockwise torque = -ve and anticlockwise = +ve)
hence the mass of the stick 
{tex}\therefore m ^ { \prime } = \frac { 10 \times 33 } { 5 } = 66 g{/tex}
Hence, the mass of the metre stick is 66 g.

subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
question_text: The oxygen molecule has a mass of {tex}5.30 \times 10 ^ { - 26 }{/tex} kg and a moment of inertia of {tex}1.94 \times 10 ^ { - 46 } \mathrm { kg } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex} about an axis through its centre perpendicular to the lines joining the two atoms. Suppose the mean speed of such a molecule in a gas is 500 m/s and that its kinetic energy of rotation is two thirds of its kinetic energy of translation. Find the average angular velocity of the molecule.
question_html: The oxygen molecule has a mass of {tex}5.30 \times 10 ^ { - 26 }{/tex} kg and a moment of inertia of {tex}1.94 \times 10 ^ { - 46 } \mathrm { kg } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex} about an axis through its centre perpendicular to the lines joining the two atoms. Suppose the mean speed of such a molecule in a gas is 500 m/s and that its kinetic energy of rotation is two thirds of its kinetic energy of translation. Find the average angular velocity of the molecule.
solution_text: Mass of an oxygen molecule, m = {tex}5.30 \times 10 ^ {-26}{/tex} kg Moment of inertia, {tex}l = 1.94 \times 10 ^ { - 46 } \mathrm { kg } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex} Velocity of the oxygen molecule, v = 500 m/s The separation between the two atoms of the oxygen molecule = 2r Mass of each oxygen atom = {tex}\frac { m } { 2 }{/tex} Hence, moment of inertia I, is calculated as: {tex}\left( \frac { m } { 2 } \right) r ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { m } { 2 } \right) r ^ { 2 } = m r ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}r = \sqrt { \frac { I } { m } }{/tex} {tex}\sqrt { \frac { 1.94 \times 10 ^ { - 45 } } { 5.36 \times 10 ^ { - 28 } } } = 0.60 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} It is given that: {tex}K E _ { r o t } = \frac { 2 } { 3 } K E _ { trave }{/tex} {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } I \omega ^ { 2 } = \frac { 2 } { 3 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times m v ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}m r ^ { 2 } \omega ^ { 2 } = \frac { 2 } { 3 } m v ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\omega = \sqrt { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \frac { v } { r }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \times \frac { 500 } { 0.6 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } }{/tex} {tex}= 6.80 \times 10 ^ { 12 } \mathrm { rad } / \mathrm { s }{/tex}
solution_html:
Mass of an oxygen molecule, m = {tex}5.30 \times 10 ^ {-26}{/tex} kg
Moment of inertia, {tex}l = 1.94 \times 10 ^ { - 46 } \mathrm { kg } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
Velocity of the oxygen molecule, v = 500 m/s
The separation between the two atoms of the oxygen molecule = 2r
Mass of each oxygen atom = {tex}\frac { m } { 2 }{/tex}
Hence, moment of inertia I, is calculated as:
{tex}\left( \frac { m } { 2 } \right) r ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { m } { 2 } \right) r ^ { 2 } = m r ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}r = \sqrt { \frac { I } { m } }{/tex}
{tex}\sqrt { \frac { 1.94 \times 10 ^ { - 45 } } { 5.36 \times 10 ^ { - 28 } } } = 0.60 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
It is given that:
{tex}K E _ { r o t } = \frac { 2 } { 3 } K E _ { trave }{/tex}
{tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } I \omega ^ { 2 } = \frac { 2 } { 3 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times m v ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}m r ^ { 2 } \omega ^ { 2 } = \frac { 2 } { 3 } m v ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\omega = \sqrt { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \frac { v } { r }{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \times \frac { 500 } { 0.6 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } }{/tex}
{tex}= 6.80 \times 10 ^ { 12 } \mathrm { rad } / \mathrm { s }{/tex}
